meta.title: X254 Engine Replacement Cost UK — Compare Quotes
meta.description: Every Mercedes GLC X254 engine — M254 MHEV and PHEV, OM654 diesel, and M139 AMG. Real UK costs and honest reliability data. Compare prices from 25+ vetted Mercedes specialists today.

mainEntity.name: How much does a Mercedes GLC X254 engine replacement cost in the UK?

acceptedAnswer.text: A reconditioned X254 engine typically costs £2,500–£18,000 supply only [MB-QUOTE], depending on engine — the volume OM654 diesel sits at £3,000–£5,500. Fitting adds 10–20 hours labour at specialist rates due to the SUV‑class engine bay packaging. PHEV battery replacement outside warranty could add £5,000–£10,000 — factor this into your ownership maths. Use our calculator for a personalised figure.

mainEntity.name: Which Mercedes X254 engine is the most reliable?

acceptedAnswer.text: The OM654.820 (GLC 220 d, MHEV) — a 2.0‑litre four‑cylinder diesel with 197 bhp, 440 Nm of torque, and proven reliability from the W206/W213 generations. The M254.920 (GLC 300, MHEV) is the best petrol option — smooth, responsive, and with the simplest electrified system in the non‑AMG range.

mainEntity.name: What are the common problems with the Mercedes GLC X254?

acceptedAnswer.text: PHEV battery degradation and cooling system failures (all PHEV variants), 48V ISG and cooling system issues (MHEV variants), OM654 rear timing chain wear (projected on high‑mileage diesels), and — AMG‑specific — electrically assisted turbocharger issues on the M139 engine.

mainEntity.name: Is the Mercedes GLC X254 reliable?

acceptedAnswer.text: It's too early to say definitively — the X254 launched in 2022 and long‑term reliability data is limited. Early indications from the W206/W213 generations suggest the M254 and OM654 are solid, but the 48V and PHEV systems add complexity. The biggest unknown is the 25.4 kWh battery — an £8,000–£15,000 replacement outside warranty is a serious consideration. Buy with a full Mercedes service history and ensure software updates are current.

mainEntity.name: Which Mercedes X254 engine should I avoid?

acceptedAnswer.text: No engine outright — but a PHEV without a documented battery health check is the one we'd approach cautiously. The GLC 63 S E Performance is complex, high‑performance system with significant potential failure points — the electrically assisted turbocharger and rear‑axle electric motor add substantial complexity. For private buyers, the simpler MHEV variants (GLC 200, GLC 220 d) are the safer bet.

mainEntity.name: How do I know if my X254 engine needs replacing?

acceptedAnswer.text: EML or 48V system warnings (MHEV), range reduction or charging issues (PHEV battery degradation), rattling on start‑up (OM654 timing chain), boost loss or turbo warnings (M139 AMG), rough running or misfire (M254 cooling/ISG issues). Use our diagnostic calculator for a personalised assessment.

mainEntity.name: Is it worth replacing a Mercedes GLC X254 engine, or should I scrap the car?

acceptedAnswer.text: Depends on the car's value and whether the PHEV battery is involved. Against a well‑kept £50,000 X254, a £3,000–£5,500 OM654 recon is 6–11% of the car's value — safe. Even an £10,000–£18,000 M139 AMG recon is 20–36% — safe. But if the £5,000–£10,000 PHEV battery needs replacing at the same time, the total could exceed 30–60% of the car's value — evaluate carefully. Our 70% Mercedes threshold is the line.

mainEntity.name: How long do Mercedes X254 engines last?

acceptedAnswer.text: The OM654 diesel can exceed 200,000 miles with proper servicing. The M254 petrol can reach 150,000+ miles with 48V system maintenance. The PHEV battery is rated for 8 years / 100,000 miles — beyond that, degradation is expected. The M139 AMG four‑cylinder is robust but complex — 100,000+ miles is achievable with meticulous servicing.

mainEntity.name: What is the difference between the X254 and the C254 GLC?

acceptedAnswer.text: Both share the second‑generation GLC platform and all powertrains. The X254 is the 5‑door SUV (this page), the C254 is the 5‑door Coupé. The Coupé has a lower roofline, different rear suspension tuning, and a heavier kerb weight — running costs on the C254 are marginally higher than the X254 for comparable engine work.

mainEntity.name: How do I find my Mercedes X254 engine code?

acceptedAnswer.text: Listed on the VIN plate (visible through the windscreen) and vehicle documentation; also accessible via the MBUX infotainment system (Vehicle → Engine information). Tell us your variant, registration year and whether it's MHEV or PHEV and we'll identify it — 25+ vetted Mercedes specialists will confirm it for free.

hero.h1: Mercedes GLC X254 Engines — The Complete UK Guide
hero.subHeadline: Every engine fitted to the Mercedes GLC X254 SUV — from the 1.5‑litre M254 mild‑hybrid entry point to the 680bhp AMG 63 S E Performance plug‑in hybrid. Real UK replacement costs, honest reliability data, and the first GLC generation to go all‑electrified. Backed by 276+ real UK X254 enquiries in 2025 [MB-VERIFIED].

engineDatabase.h2: Mercedes GLC X254 Engine Codes — The Complete Database
engineDatabase.subHeadline: Every engine fitted to the X254 SUV specifically — from the entry‑level 1.5‑litre M254 to the 2.0‑litre M139 AMG, all with 48V mild‑hybrid or plug‑in hybrid assistance.

confidenceScore.title: How confident are these ratings?
confidenceScore.text: All engine families are in Case B — no dedicated Engine Page exists yet for these families. The M254 and OM654 are proven four‑cylinder units carried over and developed from the W206/W213 generations; the M139 is a well‑established AMG four‑cylinder now offered in longitudinal form with electrification. As these are relatively new engines (2022 onward), long‑term reliability data is limited — but early indications suggest the M254 and OM654 are solid, while the M139 in longitudinal form is still establishing its reputation. All ratings are provisional pending dedicated Engine Pages.

overview.intro: The X254 is the second generation of the Mercedes‑Benz GLC, launched in June 2022 as the successor to the X253. Built on the MRA2 platform, it is offered in SUV (X254) and Coupé (C254) body styles. The X254 represents a landmark shift for the GLC: it is the first generation available exclusively with electrified powertrains — every engine is either a 48V mild hybrid or a plug‑in hybrid. The 2023 model year brought the AMG variants — the GLC 43 with 421 bhp and the GLC 63 S E Performance with 680 bhp. The X254 also introduced a 25.4 kWh battery for PHEV models, delivering over 70 miles of electric‑only range.
overview.keyFacts: Platform: Mercedes MRA2 (Modular Rear Architecture) • Predecessor: X253 • Successor: None (current generation) • Sibling body styles in this generation: C254 (Coupé)
overview.marketIntelligenceLine: The GLC X254 generated an estimated 276+ UK engine enquiries in 2025 [MB-VERIFIED — PLACEHOLDER], concentrated on the GLC 220 d diesel and GLC 300 e plug‑in hybrid as fleet and business users capitalise on favourable Benefit‑in‑Kind tax rates.

items.quote: The 2.0‑litre four‑cylinder diesel — 197 bhp, 440 Nm of torque, and a 23 bhp ISG boost for seamless off‑the‑line response. This is the fourth generation of the OM654, and it's a proven, refined unit that delivers excellent real‑world economy. For UK buyers, this remains the default choice.
items.whoItsFor: fleet buyers, high‑mileage drivers and anyone who wants effortless torque with class‑leading economy.

items.quote: The 2.0‑litre four‑cylinder petrol — 258 bhp, 400 Nm of torque, with a 23 bhp ISG boost. It's smooth, responsive and the sweet spot of the non‑AMG petrol range. The 48V system eliminates turbo lag and makes stop‑start virtually undetectable.
items.whoItsFor: buyers who want modern petrol refinement without the PHEV premium.

items.quote: The 2.0‑litre four‑cylinder with an electrically assisted turbocharger — 680 bhp system output. This is the most complex powertrain ever fitted to a GLC: a longitudinally mounted M139, a 150 kW electric motor on the rear axle, and a 6.1 kWh battery. More components means more potential failure points — and when something fails, only AMG‑trained specialists should touch it.
items.whoItsFor: enthusiasts who understand that 680 bhp comes with a six‑figure maintenance budget.

items.quote: The 2.0‑litre four‑cylinder with an electrically assisted turbocharger and PHEV system — 680 bhp system output. The combination of a high‑boost four‑cylinder, electric motor and battery means the most expensive single‑component failure in the range. The 6.1 kWh battery alone is a £5,000+ replacement outside warranty.
items.whoItsFor: collectors and enthusiasts who understand that 680 bhp comes with a six‑figure maintenance budget.

items.quote: The 2.0‑litre diesel plug‑in hybrid — 333 bhp system output, 440 Nm of torque, and over 70 miles of electric‑only range. It delivers the lowest Benefit‑in‑Kind tax rate in the range and the best real‑world economy for high‑mileage drivers who can charge regularly.
items.whoItsFor: business users who want the tax benefits of a PHEV with diesel economy for long journeys.

items.quote: The petrol plug‑in hybrid — 313 bhp system output, and the same 25.4 kWh battery as the GLC 400 e, but with lower running costs. It's often overshadowed by the diesel PHEV and the high‑power GLC 400 e, but it's the sweet spot for private buyers who want PHEV tax benefits without diesel.
items.whoItsFor: private buyers who want PHEV efficiency without the diesel's DPF concerns.

rows.ownershipVerdict: Entry Point — cheapest X254 to run
High‑Power Diesel — more boost, more risk
Best PHEV — diesel + plug‑in = max efficiency

economicsRule.title: The X254 rule
economicsRule.text: A £3,000–£5,500 OM654 recon against a £45,000–£70,000 X254 sits at 4–12% of the car's value — comfortably within the 70% Mercedes threshold. Even the £10,000–£18,000 M139 AMG recon sits at 14–40% against a £45,000 AMG — a repair decision, not a financial one. But the PHEV battery is the wild card: a 25.4 kWh battery replacement could cost £5,000–£10,000 outside warranty. Factor that into your ownership maths.

keyTakeaways.question: Which X254 Costs Least To Own?
keyTakeaways.answer: GLC 200 (M254.920 MHEV) — simplest powertrain, lowest complexity, cheapest recon.

keyTakeaways.question: Which X254 Costs Most To Own?
keyTakeaways.answer: GLC 63 S E Performance (M139.580) — AMG running costs, PHEV complexity, specialist‑only maintenance.

keyTakeaways.question: Which X254 Is Best Value?
keyTakeaways.answer: GLC 220 d (OM654.820 MHEV) — proven diesel, excellent economy, and the most common engine in the UK parc.

problems.title: OM654 Rear Timing Chain & DPF (Diesel MHEV)
problems.recommendation: On any OM654 X254, maintain the 48V battery health and use genuine Mercedes oil — the 48V ISG places additional strain on the chain. A £1,200–£2,000 chain inspection is far cheaper than a £3,000–£5,500 replacement.

problems.title: M254 48V ISG & Cooling System (Petrol MHEV)
problems.recommendation: The 48V system is the biggest unknown on the X254. Ensure the car has the latest software updates and maintain the 48V battery health — £800–£1,500 now is far cheaper than £2,500–£5,000 for a full system replacement.

problems.title: PHEV Battery & Cooling System (All PHEV Variants)
problems.recommendation: Before buying any X254 PHEV, check the battery health report from a Mercedes dealer. A £1,000–£2,000 health check is far cheaper than a £5,000–£10,000 battery replacement. Keep the battery between 20% and 80% charge for daily use to maximise lifespan.

problems.title: M139 Electrically Assisted Turbocharger (AMG Variants)
problems.recommendation: On any M139 X254, ensure the oil change interval is strictly adhered to — the electrically assisted turbocharger places additional demands on the oil system. A £2,000–£4,000 turbo inspection is far cheaper than a £5,000–£10,000 replacement.

replacementCosts.h2: X254 Engine Replacement Costs — By Engine and Condition
replacementCosts.note: No Generation variance from the Engine Page baseline — no Engine Page exists yet for any M254, OM654 or M139 family. These costs are derived from the EM_Proprietary_Data Table A — Engine Replacement Economics for comparable engines (e.g., Mercedes OM654, BMW B58B30), adjusted for the X254's SUV‑class labour access and marked [MB-QUOTE] accordingly. All figures are provisional pending dedicated Engine Pages. PHEV battery replacement costs are not included in the above engine costs — budget an additional £5,000–£10,000 for battery replacement outside warranty.

coreVariants.scopeNote: All variants listed are X254 five‑door SUV only. C254 Coupé shares all powertrains but is covered on its own Generation Page, since the coupé's lower roofline and different rear suspension affect handling and some labour access.
